era_context: The Classical Greek world in the mid-4th century BCE, with Thebes at the height of its power following Leuctra (371 BCE). Sparta's hegemony had been broken, but Theban dominance was not yet secure. Philip II of Macedon was consolidating his kingdom in the north. The Persian Achaemenid Empire remained vast but internally unstable. In China, the Warring States period was in full swing.

events.title: Theban hegemony at its peak; Epaminondas leads military campaigns
events.description: In 367 BCE, Thebes under Epaminondas was at the height of its power. Epaminondas was consolidating Theban control over Boeotia and extending influence throughout Greece. The Theban hegemony, established after the victory at Leuctra in 371 BCE, was still secure, though it would face increasing challenges from Philip II of Macedon and internal Greek rivalries. This period represents the brief moment when Thebes was the dominant Greek power.
events.certainty_note: Xenophon's Hellenica provides near-contemporary evidence, though it ends around 362 BCE. Later sources like Plutarch and Diodorus provide narrative detail but are written centuries later. The general trajectory of Theban power is secure; specific events in 367 BCE are less precisely dated.
cross_references: -362-001 (Battle of Mantinea, end of Theban hegemony)

events.title: Plato visits Syracuse; philosophical influence on Dionysius II
events.description: Plato, the Athenian philosopher, made his third and final visit to Syracuse in Sicily around 367 BCE (some sources date this to 361 BCE). He came at the invitation of Dionysius II, the young tyrant of Syracuse, hoping to influence him toward philosophical governance. The visit was ultimately unsuccessful and ended in conflict; Dionysius II eventually had Plato imprisoned or enslaved (accounts vary). This episode illustrates both the appeal of Platonic philosophy to rulers and the practical difficulties of implementing philosophical ideals in politics.
events.certainty_note: Plato's Seventh Letter is autobiographical and provides first-hand evidence, though its authenticity has been debated by scholars. The date of the visit is uncertain; some sources place it in 361 BCE. The general outline of the visit and its failure is well-attested.

events.title: Philip II of Macedon continues consolidation and expansion
events.description: Philip II was in the early-to-middle years of his reign, consolidating Macedonian power and beginning to expand northward and eastward. By 367 BCE, he had secured his throne against internal rivals and was establishing Macedon as a major regional power. His military reforms were being implemented, and he was beginning to pose a threat to the Greek city-states to the south. This period marks the beginning of Macedonian ascendancy.
events.certainty_note: The chronology of Philip's early reign is uncertain. Sources disagree on the dates of specific campaigns. The general trajectory is clear, but year-specific events are difficult to pin down.

events.title: Warring States period: military innovation and state consolidation in China
events.description: During the Warring States period, Chinese states were engaged in continuous military competition and technological innovation. Around 367 BCE, crossbow technology was being refined, and chariot tactics were being superseded by infantry formations. The state of Qin in the west was beginning to emerge as a major power. Philosophical schools like Legalism were developing in response to the chaos of the era, emphasizing strict laws and military discipline.
key_figures: Various rulers of Qin, Chu, Zhao, and other states

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian
Archaeological evidence from Warring States sites, including crossbow mechanisms

events.certainty_note: Chinese chronology for the Warring States period is uncertain by several years. The Shiji was compiled centuries later. Archaeological evidence provides material confirmation of technological development but not precise year-by-year dating.

disconfirming_evidence: The date of Plato's third visit to Syracuse is disputed. While some sources place it in 367 BCE, others argue for 361 BCE. The authenticity of Plato's Seventh Letter, which is our primary source for this visit, has been questioned by some scholars, though most modern scholars accept it as genuine. The chronology of Philip II's early reign is also debated. For China, the Warring States chronology is notoriously uncertain.
historiographic_note: The year 367 BCE is moderately well-documented for the Greek world, primarily through later compilations and Xenophon's near-contemporary Hellenica. For Persia, we rely on Greek accounts and archaeological evidence. Chinese sources are entirely post-hoc compilations with uncertain chronology. The historical record is heavily weighted toward the Mediterranean and Near East. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and much of Central Asia have no surviving written records for this year.

graph_edges.note: Theban hegemony under Epaminondas in 367 BCE led to the Battle of Mantinea (362 BCE), where Epaminondas died and Theban dominance ended.
Plato's failed attempt to influence Dionysius II contributed to the political instability in Syracuse that led to Dion's liberation attempt (356 BCE).
Philip II's consolidation of Macedonian power in the 360s BCE led to his dominance of Greece by 338 BCE.
